title: HTML Tags: The Core Idea
description: Before you build a page, you need to understand the one thing every HTML page is made of: the tag.
congratsText: You've got tags down! You now know how the browser reads every single HTML page.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Explain what an HTML tag is and what job it does
  • Tell an opening tag apart from a closing tag
  • Nest tags correctly and avoid the most common tag mistake

sections.title: What Is a Tag?
sections.lesson:
What Is a Tag?
A tag is a piece of code wrapped in angle brackets that tells the browser what something is. It's a label, not the content itself.
<p>
That's a tag. By itself it doesn't show anything on the page — it just says "what comes next is a paragraph." Tags are the building blocks every HTML page is made of.

quiz.question: What does an HTML tag actually do?
options: It labels a piece of content so the browser knows what it is
It runs a command that changes the server's files
It stores data permanently in the browser's memory
It connects the page to a database automatically

sections.title: Opening and Closing Tags
sections.lesson:
Opening and Closing Tags
Most tags come in pairs: an opening tag and a closing tag. The closing tag looks the same as the opening one, but with a forward slash.
<p>This is a paragraph.</p>
Everything between the opening tag <p> and the closing tag </p> is the content that tag applies to. Think of it like a sandwich — the tags are the bread, the content is the filling.

quiz.question: What is the difference between an opening tag and its closing tag?
options: The closing tag has a forward slash before the tag name
The closing tag is always written in capital letters
The closing tag comes before the content, not after
The closing tag needs a different word than the opening one

sections.title: The Anatomy of a Tag
sections.lesson:
The Anatomy of a Tag
A full tag "unit" has three parts: the opening tag, the content, and the closing tag.
<h1>Hello World</h1>
  • <h1> — opening tag, marks the start
  • Hello World — the content itself
  • </h1> — closing tag, marks the end
This whole group is called an element. The tags are the markers; the element is the tags plus everything inside them.

quiz.question: In the code below, what counts as the 'content' of the element?
options: Hello World
The entire line of code, brackets included

sections.title: Tags That Don't Close
sections.lesson:
Tags That Don't Close
Not every tag comes in a pair. Some tags represent something that has no content to wrap around, like a line break or an image. These are called void tags, or self-closing tags.
<br> <hr> <img src="cat.jpg">
A line break doesn't have "content" to close around, so there's no </br>. If a tag doesn't hold text or other tags inside it, it usually doesn't need a closing tag.

quiz.question:
Why does the
tag not need a closing tag?
options: It has no content inside it to wrap around
Browsers ignore closing tags for short tag names
It is not technically part of the HTML language
Closing tags are only required inside a <form>

sections.title: Nesting Tags
sections.lesson:
Nesting Tags
Tags can go inside other tags. This is called nesting. The tag on the outside is the parent; the tag on the inside is the child.
<p>This word is <strong>important</strong>.</p>
Here, <strong> is nested inside <p>. The rule is simple: whatever tag you open last, you must close first — like stacking boxes inside boxes.

quiz.question:
Which line correctly nests inside

?
options: This is important.
This is important.
This is important.
This is important.

sections.title: Where Attributes Live
sections.lesson:
Where Attributes Live
A tag can carry extra information called an attribute. Attributes always go inside the opening tag only — never the closing tag.
<a href="https://example.com">Click here</a>
Here, href is the attribute name and "https://example.com" is its value. The closing tag </a> stays plain — it never repeats the attribute.

quiz.question: Where does an attribute like href belong in an element?
options: Inside the opening tag only
Inside the closing tag only
Split between the opening and closing tag
Anywhere between the two tags

sections.title: Tag Names Are Lowercase
sections.lesson:
Tag Names Are Lowercase
HTML tag names are not case-sensitive to the browser — <P> and <p> both work. But the standard, expected style is to always write tag names in lowercase.
<p>Good practice</p> <P>Works, but avoid this</P>
Sticking to lowercase keeps your code consistent and easier to read, especially once your files get longer.

quiz.question:
What is true about the tag

written in uppercase?
options: The browser still renders it, but lowercase is the accepted convention
It throws an error and the browser refuses to load the page
It behaves like a completely different tag than
It only works inside the <head> section

sections.title: The Most Common Mistake
sections.lesson:
The Most Common Mistake
The single most common tag mistake is forgetting to close a tag, or closing tags out of order.
<p>Text that never ends... <p>Another paragraph</p>
Here the first <p> was never closed. Browsers often try to guess what you meant and still show something — but the layout can end up wrong in ways that are hard to spot. Always check that every opening tag has a matching closing tag in the right order.

quiz.question: What usually happens when a tag is left unclosed?
options: The browser tries to guess the structure, which can break the layout
The page refuses to load at all until it's fixed
The browser automatically deletes that section of content
Nothing changes — closing tags are only for documentation

sections.title: Recap: Reading Any Tag
sections.lesson:
Recap: Reading Any Tag
Every tag you meet from here on breaks down the same way:
  • Angle brackets mark where a tag starts and ends
  • Most tags pair up: opening tag, content, closing tag
  • Some tags are void — no content, no closing tag
  • Tags can nest inside other tags, closing in reverse order
  • Attributes live in the opening tag only
<div> <p>Some <a href="#">linked</a> text.</p> </div>
Once tags click, the rest of HTML is just learning which tag to use for which job — and soon, how to collect input back from the person reading the page.

quiz.question: Which statement correctly summarizes how HTML tags work together?
options: Tags mark content, can nest, and close in the reverse order they were opened
Every tag must always have both a closing tag and an attribute
Nesting is optional and tags never need to close in a specific order
Attributes replace the need for a separate closing tag
